one_line_takeaway: SAC replaces sender-reported confidence with receiver-side, MSR-style filtering on (F+1)-robust communication graphs, closing the self-confidence-falsification attack that collapses the CP-WBFT baseline and turning negative Byzantine Fault Tolerance Improvement into consistently non-negative-to-positive BFTI across math, commonsense, and open-weight-model settings.
key_finding: Across all tested settings, CP-WBFT (the paper's main baseline) produces consistently NEGATIVE BFTI when facing an adversarial agent with a falsified self-confidence of 1.0 -- MATH: -8.7% to -17.5%; Commonsense170k: -32.4% to -54.7%; open-weight five-benchmark average: -16.5% to -26.1% -- typically because strong-agent accuracy collapses within the first communication round while it is dragged toward the adversarial agent's answer. SAC instead achieves non-negative-to-positive BFTI in nearly every configuration -- MATH: +0.4% to +3.0%; Commonsense170k: -2.8% to +0.0% (near-zero degradation); open-weight average: +3.2% to +3.9% -- while lifting weak-agent accuracy without dragging down strong-agent accuracy. H-Majority for SAC reaches 78-79% (MATH), 76.7-83.3% (Commonsense170k) and 77.2-78.4% (open-weight, vs. 38.8-40.0% for CP-WBFT). Ablations (Table 5, n=7, varying adversarial-to-weak composition) show SAC keeps H-Majority at 83.3-96.7% versus CP-WBFT's consistently negative BFTI; SAC also scales to n=9 (Table 6) maintaining positive BFTI on 3 of 4 topologies and H-Majority of 86.7-90.0%, including on a 4-robust preferential-attachment graph, described by the authors as scaling 'without retuning.'
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (distinct from the Related Work it cites) is twofold: (1) Self-Anchored Consensus (SAC), a fully decentralized, MSR-inspired (Mean-Subsequence-Reduced) filter-and-refine protocol for LLM multi-agent systems in which each agent computes RECEIVER-SIDE evaluation scores of its neighbors' responses (via its own LLM acting as a verifier, rather than trusting a sender-reported confidence score), discards the bottom-F lowest-scoring neighbor responses relative to its own score, and then re-prompts itself to refine its answer using its own prior response plus the retained, score-weighted neighbor responses, iterated for T rounds; and (2) a graph-theoretic design condition -- (F+1)-robustness, built on LeBlanc et al.'s r-robustness notion from classical Byzantine-resilient consensus -- under which the authors prove (Property 4.2 and 4.3, with proofs given in the paper) that after F-filtering each honest agent retains at least one neighbor response no worse in quality than the worst reliable agent's response, and that the filtered directed graph still contains a rooted out-branching, preventing network fragmentation. The paper explicitly distinguishes its method from three closest prior works it treats as baselines/related work only: CP-WBFT (Zheng et al. 2026, self-reported confidence, ad hoc topology choice), Trusted MultiLLMN (Luo et al. 2025, leader-based BFT with expensive re-elections), and DecentLLMs (Jo and Park 2025, geometric-mean filter requiring a fully-connected evaluator-worker graph) -- none of these are SAC or claimed as the paper's own method.
threat_model: Formal Byzantine-agent threat model adapted from LeBlanc et al. [2013] (Def. 3.1-3.2) for a peer-to-peer network of n LLM agents G=(V,E) solving queries with objectively verifiable ground-truth answers. Agents in the Byzantine set B are split into: Faulty Byzantine (F-subset) agents that follow the protocol but produce incorrect/noisy outputs due to hallucination, miscalculation, or capability limits; and Adversarial Byzantine (A-subset) agents that deviate arbitrarily from the protocol, may send inconsistent/inconsistent-per-neighbor information, and can attempt to manipulate the system, e.g. by falsifying self-reported confidence scores (the specific attack the paper instantiates experimentally: adversarial agents return out-of-distribution weak-model responses paired with a falsified self-confidence of 1.0). The scope of Byzantine agents is bounded via the standard F-local model (Def. 3.2): every honest node has at most F Byzantine neighbors. The goal (Problem Statement, Sec 3.4) is for reliable agents P to remain robust to Byzantine messages and for all honest agents H = P union F to converge toward the correct answer despite Byzantine influence, in a fully decentralized setting where no agent knows the global topology or Byzantine identities.
limitations: The paper does not include a dedicated Limitations section; caveats are stated in Discussion/Conclusion and inferable from the setup: (1) The Byzantine model in experiments is narrow -- a single adversarial agent per network (b up to 3 only tested in ablation at n=7, and always with a fixed falsified-confidence-of-1.0 attack strategy); no adaptive or stronger/more numerous adversarial strategies (e.g., colluding adversaries crafting content-level attacks rather than just confidence-score falsification) are evaluated. (2) SAC's own robustness guarantee (Property 4.2/4.3) is a graph-theoretic/algorithmic property conditional on (F+1)-robust topology construction and on the F-local bound holding; it does not certify correctness of the receiver-side scoring function phi_i itself, which is realized by an LLM verification prompt and could itself be miscalibrated or manipulated by adversarial input content (only the self-reporting-confidence attack vector is closed). (3) SAC does occasionally show slightly negative BFTI (e.g., Commonsense170k MERG topology, -1.9%; n=9 Erdos-Renyi, -2.2%), meaning the protocol is not unconditionally safe in every configuration. (4) Evaluation is restricted to tasks with objectively verifiable ground-truth answers (math and commonsense QA); the authors do not test open-ended or safety-judgment tasks where correctness is not crisply defined. (5) r-robust topologies trade sparsity for fault tolerance and directly govern per-round inference cost (more edges = more LLM calls per round); the paper notes this trade-off but does not report wall-clock/token-cost comparisons between topologies.

datasets_used: Hendrycks MATH test set (100 sampled Level 4 problems for main results; 30 sampled Level 4 problems for ablations/scaling)
Commonsense170k (Hu et al. 2023 LLM-Adapters collection; 30 sampled instances)
GSM8K (10 hand-curated problems, used in the CP-WBFT evaluation protocol that SAC's protocol extends)
quantitative_results: MATH: CP-WBFT BFTI -8.7% to -17.5%; SAC BFTI +0.4% to +3.0% (Table 1)
MATH: SAC H-Majority 78-79% across MERG/Complete/Erdos-Renyi topologies (Table 1)
Commonsense170k: CP-WBFT BFTI -32.4% to -54.7%; SAC BFTI -2.8% to +0.0% (Table 1)
Open-weight 5-benchmark average: CP-WBFT BFTI -16.5% to -26.1%; SAC BFTI +3.2% to +3.9% (Table 3)
Open-weight 5-benchmark average: SAC H-Majority 77.2-78.4% vs CP-WBFT 38.8-40.0% (Table 3)
Ablation (n=7, varying b/w composition, MATH 30-sample): SAC H-Majority 83.3-96.7%, CP-WBFT negative BFTI in all settings (Table 5)
Scaling to n=9 (MATH 30-sample): SAC positive BFTI on 3 of 4 topologies (+1.8% to +3.0%), H-Majority 86.7-90.0%, including on a 4-robust preferential-attachment graph (Table 6)
baselines_compared: CP-WBFT (Zheng et al. 2026) -- primary empirical baseline, self-reported confidence weighting
Trusted MultiLLMN (Luo et al. 2025) -- discussed as related work, leader-based BFT, not empirically compared
DecentLLMs (Jo and Park 2025) -- discussed as related work, geometric-mean evaluator-worker filter, not empirically compared
